questions.subject: Computer Science
questions.title: What is the difference between process and thread?
questions.body: I keep confusing these two. My professor explained it but I still don't get it. Can someone explain with a simple real-world example?

answers.text: A process is an independent program with its own memory space. A thread is a unit of execution within a process, sharing the same memory. Think of a process as a restaurant and threads as the waiters working simultaneously!

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.title: How to solve integration by parts? Any trick to remember?
questions.body: Especially the ILATE rule — when exactly do I apply it and when not?

answers.text: Remember LIATE: Logarithm, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential. The first function in LIATE that appears becomes 'u', and the other becomes 'dv'. Practice 5 problems daily!

questions.subject: Physics
questions.title: Why is the sky blue? What's the actual physics behind it?

answers.text: It's Rayleigh scattering! Shorter blue wavelengths scatter more than red ones when sunlight hits the atmosphere. So wherever you look, blue reaches your eye.
At sunset, light travels through more atmosphere so most blue scatters away, leaving red and orange. That's why sunsets are so beautiful!

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.title: How do I balance redox reactions? I am so lost.
questions.body: I know the half-reaction method exists but I always get the wrong answer in the exam.

questions.subject: Economics
questions.title: What's the difference between GDP and GNP?

answers.text: GDP = value of goods/services produced within a country's borders. GNP = GDP + income by residents abroad - income by foreigners inside. GDP measures location, GNP measures nationals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.title: What is the difference between mitosis and meiosis?
questions.body: Both involve cell division but I get confused about which produces how many cells.

answers.text: Mitosis produces 2 identical daughter cells (for growth & repair). Meiosis produces 4 genetically unique cells (for sexual reproduction). Simple rule: Mitosis = More of the same. Meiosis = Mix it up!
